form.name: NGS Original Practice Test 1
form.description: An original, NGS-authored full-length adaptive practice test — every item is original (not from any real exam), mapped to the official Digital SAT skills and difficulty.

questions.stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
questions.explanation:
The passage centers on how the axolotl differs from typical salamanders by keeping its gills and staying in water for life, a trait described as remaining in a "baby" form. Choice A captures this main idea. B misstates a detail (the comparison is about other salamanders, not their city preferences), C introduces a disagreement never mentioned, and D directly contradicts the text, which says the axolotl keeps its gills.

【中文】文章的核心是说明蝾螈中的美西螈与普通蝾螈不同：它终生保留鳃并生活在水中，被形容为始终保持"幼体"形态。选项A概括了这一中心思想。B歪曲了细节（比较的是其他蝾螈的特征，而非它们对城市的偏好）；C引入了文中从未提及的分歧；D则与文意相反，因为文中说美西螈保留鳃。
questions.passage: The axolotl is a type of salamander that lives in lakes near Mexico City. Unlike most salamanders, which lose their gills and move onto land as adults, the axolotl keeps its feathery external gills and stays in the water for its entire life. Because of this unusual trait, scientists often describe the axolotl as remaining in a "baby" form even after it is fully grown.

choices.text: The axolotl is unusual because it keeps youthful, water-based features throughout its adult life.
Most salamanders prefer to live in lakes near large cities.
Scientists disagree about whether the axolotl is truly a salamander.
The axolotl loses its gills shortly before it reaches adulthood.

questions.stem: According to the text, why did restaurants agree to accept McNamara's card?
questions.explanation:
The text states directly that "restaurants accepted the card because it brought in more business," making B correct. A is never mentioned. C contradicts the passage, which says the card let diners pay without cash. D is wrong because the card was described as cardboard, not plastic, and durability is not given as the reason.

【中文】文章明确指出"餐厅接受这张卡是因为它带来了更多生意"，因此B正确。A文中从未提及。C与文意相反，因为卡片让顾客无需用现金付款。D错误，文中称卡片是纸板做的，而非塑料，且耐用性也不是被接受的原因。
questions.passage: In 1958, engineer Frank McNamara introduced a small cardboard card that let diners at certain restaurants pay for meals without using cash. Customers signed for their food and were billed later. Restaurants accepted the card because it brought in more business, and customers liked the convenience of not carrying money. This simple idea grew into the modern credit card system used around the world today.

choices.text: It allowed them to charge customers higher prices.
It attracted more customers and increased their business.
It required customers to pay entirely in cash.
It was made of a durable plastic material.

questions.stem: Based on the text, which choice is most strongly supported regarding a bee that performs a very short waggle?
questions.explanation:
The text says "the longer the bee waggles, the farther away the food is." By logical extension, a very short waggle signals that the food is relatively close, so A is supported. B is wrong because the bee is dancing, which means it did find food. C contradicts the text, which says other bees watch and respond. D introduces sunset, which is unrelated; the sun is mentioned only for direction.

【中文】文章说"蜜蜂摆动的时间越长，食物就越远"。据此推断，摆动时间很短意味着食物相对较近，所以A得到支持。B错误，因为蜜蜂在跳舞说明它确实找到了食物。C与文意相反，文中说其他蜜蜂会观察并作出反应。D引入了日落这一无关概念；太阳在文中只用于指示方向。
questions.passage: Honeybees communicate the location of food through a movement called the waggle dance. A returning bee moves in a figure-eight pattern, and the angle of its dance relative to the vertical comb shows the direction of the flowers compared to the sun. The longer the bee waggles, the farther away the food is. Other bees watch closely and then fly off in search of the source.

choices.text: The food source it found is located relatively close to the hive.
The bee was unable to locate any flowers at all.
The other bees will ignore its dance entirely.
The food source can only be reached after sunset.

questions.stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?
questions.explanation:
Since light colors keep homes cooler by reflecting sunlight, a logical inference is that residents who switch to lighter shades would need less cooling and therefore spend less on it. A follows directly. B contradicts the logic, since lighter homes absorb less sunlight. C and D introduce ideas (repainting frequency, relocating) that the text gives no basis for.

【中文】由于浅色通过反射阳光使房屋更凉爽，合理的推断是：改用浅色的居民需要更少的降温，因此花费更少。A直接成立。B与逻辑相反，因为浅色房屋吸收的阳光更少。C和D引入了文中没有依据的概念（重新粉刷的频率、搬迁）。
questions.passage: Researchers studying a coastal town noticed that homes painted in light colors stayed noticeably cooler inside during summer than homes painted in dark colors. Light surfaces reflect much of the sunlight that strikes them, while dark surfaces absorb it and turn it into heat. The researchers suggested that residents who repainted their dark houses in lighter shades might ______

choices.text: spend less on cooling their homes during hot months.
find that their homes absorb more sunlight than before.
need to repaint their homes more often than neighbors.
prefer to live in colder coastal regions instead.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the club's claim?
questions.explanation:
The club's claim is that reusable trays reduce waste. A finding that trash dropped sharply after the switch directly supports that claim. B concerns weight, not waste reduction. C describes a controlled condition but does not by itself show a reduction in waste. D is irrelevant to the amount of garbage produced.

【中文】俱乐部的主张是可重复使用的托盘能减少垃圾。如果发现更换后垃圾量大幅下降，这就直接支持了该主张，所以A正确。B涉及重量，而非垃圾减少。C描述了一个受控条件，但本身并不能证明垃圾减少了。D与产生的垃圾量无关。
questions.passage: A student club claimed that switching the school cafeteria to reusable trays would reduce waste. To test the idea, the club replaced disposable trays with reusable ones for one month and measured the trash collected each day. They wanted to show that the change had a real effect on the amount of garbage the cafeteria produced.

choices.text: The daily amount of cafeteria trash dropped sharply after reusable trays were introduced.
Students said the reusable trays were heavier than the disposable ones.
The cafeteria served the same meals during the test month as before.
Reusable trays came in several different colors.

questions.stem: Which quotation from a resident would best illustrate Reyes's argument?
questions.explanation:
Reyes argues that nearby parks make people more likely to walk and enjoy the trip rather than drive. Quotation A shows exactly this: a resident who walks to the store and enjoys it because of a new park. B is about buying a car, unrelated to parks. C concerns crowding, not the choice to walk. D describes a preference for driving, which works against the argument.

【中文】Reyes认为附近的公园使人们更愿意步行并享受出行，而不是开车。引语A正好说明了这一点：一位居民因为新建的公园而步行去商店并乐在其中。B讲的是买车，与公园无关。C涉及拥挤，而非步行的选择。D描述了对开车的偏好，与论点相悖。
questions.passage: In her essay on city design, urban planner Dahlia Reyes argues that adding small neighborhood parks makes residents more likely to walk rather than drive for short trips. She points out that when people have pleasant green spaces nearby, the walk itself becomes part of the enjoyment of the trip, encouraging them to leave their cars at home.

choices.text: "Now that there's a little park on my street, I enjoy strolling to the store instead of driving."
"I bought a new car last year because my old one broke down."
"The parks in our city are usually crowded on weekends."
"I prefer driving everywhere because it saves me time."

questions.stem: Which choice best uses the data to support the librarian's point?
questions.explanation:
The librarian wants to highlight the single section with the largest share of borrowing. Fiction's 320 books is the highest of the four figures, so A directly supports the point. B identifies the smallest section, the opposite of the goal. C and D give accurate sums but do not identify the single largest section.

【中文】图书管理员想强调借阅量占比最大的单一类别。小说类的320本是四个数字中最高的，因此A直接支持了这一观点。B指出的是最小的类别，与目标相反。C和D给出的是准确的合计数，但并未指出借阅量最大的单一类别。
questions.passage: A library tracked how many books were borrowed in each of four sections during one week. The results were: Fiction, 320 books; Science, 145 books; History, 90 books; and Art, 60 books. A librarian reviewing the data wanted to highlight which single section accounted for the largest share of the week's borrowing.

choices.text: The Fiction section, with 320 books borrowed, accounted for the most borrowing of any section.
The Art section had the fewest books borrowed, with 60.
The Science and History sections together were borrowed 235 times.
All four sections combined accounted for 615 books borrowed.

questions.stem: Which choice most accurately uses the data to complete the teacher's claim that the majority of surveyed students preferred ______
questions.explanation:
Of 200 students, 110 chose group studying, which is more than half (over 100), so it is the majority preference. A states this correctly. B mismatches the number 110 with studying alone (which was 55). C wrongly calls flashcards the most popular (only 35). D contradicts the data by saying fewer than half chose group studying, when 110 of 200 is a majority.

【中文】在200名学生中，110人选择小组学习，超过一半（多于100人），因此这是多数人的偏好。A正确陈述了这一点。B把数字110错误地配给了独自学习（实际为55人）。C错误地称卡片是最受欢迎的（仅35人）。D与数据相矛盾，说选择小组学习的人不到一半，而110/200是多数。
questions.passage: A teacher surveyed students about their favorite way to study for tests. Out of 200 students, 110 said they preferred studying with a group, 55 said they preferred studying alone, and 35 said they liked using flashcards. The teacher wanted to make a claim about the study method most students favored.

choices.text: studying in a group, chosen by more than half of the students surveyed.
studying alone, chosen by 110 of the students surveyed.
using flashcards, the most popular method in the survey.
studying in a group, though fewer than half of students chose it.

questions.stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
questions.explanation:
The passage centers on how the bobtail squid avoids casting a detectable shadow by hosting glowing bacteria that emit a downward light matching the moon, erasing its silhouette. Choice A captures this central idea. B overstates Dr. Nair's role (she studies it, not necessarily 'first'). C describes other animals only as a contrast, not the main point. D is a minor detail, not the main idea.

【中文】文章的核心是短尾鱿鱼如何通过寄宿发光细菌、向下发出与月光亮度匹配的光来消除自己的影子，从而避免被下方的猎物察觉。选项A概括了这一中心思想。B夸大了Nair博士的角色（她研究它，但未必是“第一个”）。C只是把其他动物作为对比，并非主旨。D是次要细节，而非主要观点。
questions.passage: Marine biologist Dr. Priya Nair studies the bobtail squid, a thumb-sized creature that hunts at night in shallow Pacific waters. Unlike many sea animals that hide in the dark, the bobtail squid would normally cast a visible shadow on the seafloor under moonlight, alerting prey below. To avoid this, the squid hosts colonies of glowing bacteria in a special organ and emits a soft downward light that matches the brightness of the moon overhead. By erasing its own silhouette, the squid moves through the water column undetected.

choices.text: The bobtail squid uses glowing bacteria to produce light that hides its shadow from prey beneath it.
Dr. Nair is the first scientist to observe the hunting habits of the bobtail squid at night.
Most sea animals avoid detection by hiding in dark waters rather than producing light.
The bobtail squid is unusually small compared with other squid species in the Pacific.

questions.stem: According to the text, what was one result of Caldwell's system?
questions.explanation:
The text states that neighboring libraries, impressed by the results, 'adopted her method within a decade,' and that it became a model for training programs. Choice A reflects this result. B was the problem that prompted her system, not a result of it. C is never stated. D contradicts the text, which says her approach became a model FOR training programs.

【中文】文章指出，邻近的图书馆对效果印象深刻，“在十年内采用了她的方法”，并成为培训项目的范本。选项A反映了这一结果。B是促使她设计系统的问题，而非系统带来的结果。C文中从未提及。D与文章矛盾，文中说她的方法成为培训项目的范本。
questions.passage: In the 1890s, librarian Edith Caldwell faced a growing problem: as her town's collection swelled past ten thousand volumes, patrons struggled to locate books shelved only by the date of purchase. Caldwell devised a system that grouped books by subject and assigned each a code combining a topic number with the first letters of the author's surname. Neighboring libraries, impressed by how quickly visitors found materials, adopted her method within a decade. Her approach reduced the average search time so dramatically that it became a model for regional library training programs.

choices.text: Nearby libraries began using a similar organizational method.
Her town's collection grew to more than ten thousand volumes.
Patrons began purchasing more books for the library.
Libraries stopped offering training programs for staff.

questions.stem: Which choice most logically completes the text with a conclusion about the warming intervals?
questions.explanation:
The text presents a puzzle: the warming intervals waste energy, yet squirrels denied them suffer neurological damage. The most logical inference is that the intervals, though costly, serve a necessary protective function. Choice A captures this. B contradicts the damage evidence. C ignores the damage that results from eliminating them. D misattributes the damage to cold itself rather than the absence of warming intervals.

【中文】文章提出一个谜题：升温间歇浪费能量，但被剥夺这一过程的松鼠会出现神经损伤。最合逻辑的推断是，这些间歇虽然消耗能量，却起着必要的保护作用。选项A体现了这一点。B与损伤证据矛盾。C忽视了取消间歇所导致的损伤。D错误地把损伤归因于低温本身，而非缺少升温间歇。
questions.passage: The arctic ground squirrel survives brutal winters by entering a state in which its body temperature drops below the freezing point of water, yet its blood does not turn to ice. Researchers note that the squirrel periodically rouses itself for a few hours, raising its temperature before sinking back into deep cold. These warming intervals consume large amounts of stored energy, which seems counterproductive for an animal trying to conserve resources through the winter. Yet squirrels prevented from these intervals show signs of neurological damage by spring.

choices.text: The warming intervals, despite their energy cost, likely serve a function necessary for the squirrel's survival.
The warming intervals are an evolutionary accident with no measurable effect on the squirrel.
The squirrel would conserve more energy if it eliminated the warming intervals entirely.
Neurological damage in squirrels is caused primarily by low body temperatures.

questions.stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?
questions.explanation:
The clues point to a method other than hooks or nets: the settlers had seaworthy boats with compartments and large sealed baskets matching those compartments, plus deep-water fish bones. The logical inference is that they reached offshore grounds by boat and used the baskets to store or trap fish. Choice A integrates all the evidence. B (inland trade) ignores the boats and offshore fish. C contradicts the boats and offshore catch. D ignores the fishing-related basket evidence.

【中文】线索指向一种不依赖鱼钩或大网的方法：定居者拥有带隔间的适航船只、与隔间尺寸匹配的密封大篮子，以及深海鱼骨。合逻辑的推断是他们乘船到达离岸渔场，并用篮子储存或捕捉鱼。选项A整合了所有证据。B（内陆贸易）忽视了船只和离岸鱼。C与船只和离岸渔获矛盾。D忽视了与捕鱼相关的篮子证据。
questions.passage: Archaeologists excavating a 3,000-year-old coastal settlement found large quantities of fish bones from species that live only in deep offshore waters, far from the shoreline. The settlement's tools, however, included no hooks or large nets suited to deep-sea fishing. Among the artifacts, researchers did uncover the remains of several sturdy wooden vessels and fragments of woven baskets coated in a tar-like sealant. These baskets were too large to carry comfortably by hand but matched the dimensions of compartments built into the recovered boats.

choices.text: the settlers most likely traveled by boat to fishing grounds and used the sealed baskets to store or trap their catch.
the deep-water fish bones were probably brought to the site through trade with inland communities.
the settlers preferred shoreline fishing because they lacked any means of reaching deeper waters.
the wooden vessels were used primarily for transporting people rather than for any fishing activity.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Romano's claim that the pitch adjustment is a flexible behavioral response?
questions.explanation:
Romano claims the pitch change is a flexible behavioral response, not an inherited trait. The text says individual sparrows moved between environments should change pitch. Choice A directly shows this: rural birds moved to the city raised their pitch, demonstrating flexibility. B suggests a genetic (fixed) basis, undermining her claim. C suggests pitch is inherited from parents, again undermining flexibility. D establishes a noise trend but says nothing about whether individuals can adjust.

【中文】Romano认为音高变化是一种灵活的行为反应，而非遗传特征。文中说在不同环境间迁移的个体应当改变音高。选项A直接证明了这一点：迁入城市的乡村鸟提高了音高，展示了灵活性。B暗示遗传（固定）基础，削弱其论点。C暗示音高来自父母遗传，同样削弱灵活性。D只说明噪音趋势，未涉及个体能否调整。
questions.passage: In her study of urban birdsong, ornithologist Lucia Romano proposed that city-dwelling sparrows sing at higher pitches than their rural relatives because low-frequency traffic noise would otherwise mask their calls. To support this, she argued that the adjustment is a flexible behavioral response rather than a fixed trait inherited at birth. If Romano is correct, then individual sparrows moved between environments should change the pitch of their songs accordingly.

choices.text: Rural sparrows relocated to noisy city neighborhoods raised the pitch of their songs within several weeks.
City sparrows and rural sparrows belong to genetically distinct populations that rarely interbreed.
Sparrows raised in silent laboratory conditions sang at pitches similar to those of their parents.
Traffic noise in cities has increased steadily over the past several decades.

questions.stem: Which finding from the records would most strongly support the historian's argument?
questions.explanation:
The historian argues the decline was caused by harbor silting that blocked large ships, an economic cause. Choice A directly supports this: large vessels bypassed Velmar for deeper harbors and trade tax revenue fell, linking the harbor problem to economic collapse. B describes earlier prosperity, not the cause of decline. C points to disease, which she explicitly rejected. D points to warfare, which she also rejected.

【中文】这位历史学家认为衰落源于河港淤塞、阻挡大船进入，属经济原因。选项A直接支持：大船绕开Velmar转往更深的港口，贸易税收锐减，将港口问题与经济崩溃联系起来。B描述的是此前的繁荣，而非衰落原因。C指向疾病，她明确排除了。D指向战争，她也予以排除。
questions.passage: A team of historians debated why a medieval trading town named Velmar declined sharply after 1340. One member argued that the decline was driven primarily by the silting up of the river harbor, which made it impossible for large merchant ships to dock. She maintained that economic factors, not disease or warfare, best explained the town's collapse. To test this view, the team examined tax records, shipping logs, and accounts written by visiting traders.

choices.text: Shipping logs show that after 1340, large vessels increasingly bypassed Velmar for ports with deeper harbors, and tax revenue from trade fell sharply.
Visiting traders described Velmar in 1339 as one of the busiest and wealthiest harbors in the region.
Tax records indicate that a severe outbreak of illness struck Velmar in the years after 1340.
Accounts mention that a neighboring kingdom launched several military raids on the region before 1340.

questions.stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the text to support the researchers' conclusion?
questions.explanation:
The conclusion is that some mulches greatly outperform others, with one standing out as most effective. Wood-chip mulch at 42% is the highest, more than double bare soil's 18%, supporting both 'most effective' and the large gap. Choice A is accurate and supportive. B is false (straw's 27% is not the lowest mulch; actually shredded-leaf at 27% is lowest mulch, but B mislabels it as standing out). C is false (wood-chip 42% is highest, not straw). D is false (bare soil 18% is the lowest, not highest).

【中文】结论是某些覆盖物远胜于其他，且有一种最为有效。木屑覆盖物为42%，是最高值，超过裸土18%的两倍，既支持“最有效”，又体现差距之大。选项A准确且具支持力。B错误。C错误（木屑42%最高，而非稻草）。D错误（裸土18%最低，而非最高）。
questions.passage: Researchers tested how four types of mulch affected soil moisture in a community garden during a dry summer. They measured the percentage of soil moisture retained two weeks after watering. The results were: bare soil (no mulch), 18%; straw mulch, 31%; wood-chip mulch, 42%; and shredded-leaf mulch, 27%. The researchers concluded that some mulch types are far more effective than others at helping soil retain moisture during dry periods, with one type standing out as the most effective.

choices.text: Wood-chip mulch retained 42% soil moisture, more than twice the 18% retained by bare soil.
Shredded-leaf mulch retained 27% soil moisture, the lowest of any mulch tested.
Straw mulch retained 31% soil moisture, the highest value among the four conditions.
Bare soil retained 18% soil moisture, more than any of the three mulch types.

questions.stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the text to illustrate the researcher's observation about smaller increases at the highest reading levels?
questions.explanation:
The observation is that gains kept rising but the rate of increase shrank at higher reading levels. Choice A shows exactly this: the jump from 10 to 20 minutes is 7 points, but from 20 to 30 minutes only 2 points, demonstrating a smaller increase at the top. B shows overall difference but not the diminishing rate. C shows increasing/steady increments, not a smaller one. D states the highest value but does not illustrate the shrinking increase.

【中文】观察的核心是收益持续上升，但在较高阅读时长时增幅变小。选项A正好说明这一点：从10到20分钟增加7分，而从20到30分钟仅增加2分，体现了高端增幅缩小。B只显示总体差异，未体现增幅递减。C显示增幅持平或上升，并非缩小。D仅指出最高值，未说明增幅变小。
questions.passage: An education researcher studied whether short daily reading sessions improved vocabulary scores among middle-school students over one semester. Students were placed in four groups based on daily reading time. Average vocabulary-score gains were: 0 minutes per day, 3 points; 10 minutes per day, 9 points; 20 minutes per day, 16 points; and 30 minutes per day, 18 points. The researcher noted that score gains rose with reading time but that the increase in gains became smaller at the highest reading levels.

choices.text: Gains rose by 7 points when moving from 10 to 20 minutes but by only 2 points when moving from 20 to 30 minutes.
Students reading 0 minutes per day gained 3 points, while those reading 30 minutes gained 18 points.
Gains rose by 6 points when moving from 0 to 10 minutes and by 7 points when moving from 10 to 20 minutes.
Students reading 30 minutes per day achieved the highest gain of any group, at 18 points.

questions.stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?
questions.explanation:
The passage's central claim is Venkat's reframing: the surplus fat 'functions less as fuel than as insurance,' a buffer the birds 'rarely fully expend.' Choice A captures both halves—safety margin, not typically consumed. B contradicts 'rarely fully expend.' C overstates 'impractical'; the route is 'survivable.' D introduces a claim (less fat would suffice) the text never makes; the buffer's value lies precisely in being held in reserve.

【中文】文章的核心观点是 Venkat 的重新诠释：多余的脂肪“与其说是燃料，不如说是保险”，是一种鸟类“很少完全耗尽”的缓冲。选项 A 同时抓住了两点——它是安全余量，且通常不会被耗尽。B 与“很少完全耗尽”矛盾。C 夸大为“不切实际”，而文中说该路线是“可生存的”。D 引入了文中从未提出的说法（更少脂肪就够用）；缓冲的价值恰恰在于被保留作储备。
questions.passage: In studying the migratory routes of the bar-tailed godwit, ornithologist Priya Venkat noted a paradox. The birds undertake nonstop transoceanic flights of more than 11,000 kilometers, yet they depart only after fattening to nearly double their lean body mass. Venkat argues that this extreme pre-departure fueling is not merely about endurance. Because the godwits cannot feed or rest over open ocean, even a small error in timing or fat reserves would be fatal; the surplus, she contends, functions less as fuel than as insurance, a buffer that the birds rarely fully expend but that converts an otherwise reckless gamble into a survivable, if narrow, undertaking.

choices.text: Venkat maintains that the godwits' weight gain primarily serves as a safety margin against unpredictable conditions rather than as fuel that is typically used up.
Venkat has determined that godwits routinely exhaust their entire fat reserve during transoceanic flights.
Venkat believes that the godwits' inability to feed over open ocean makes their migratory route impractical.
Venkat's research shows that godwits could complete their flights with substantially less stored fat.

questions.stem: According to the text, what is the main point Okonkwo makes about the early effects of the canals?
questions.explanation:
Okonkwo concedes canals cut freight costs but argues that in the first decade most tonnage was local exchange, so the canals 'initially deepened regional bonds before they ever dissolved them.' B states exactly this. A contradicts his explicit concession that canals lowered costs. C is wrong—he cites tonnage data showing substantial local shipping. D restates the conventional view he is rebutting.

【中文】Okonkwo 承认运河降低了运费，但主张在头十年里大部分货运量是本地交换，因此运河“最初加深了区域联系，之后才将其消解”。B 正是这一点。A 与他明确承认运河降低成本相矛盾。C 错误——他引用货运量数据显示本地运输量很大。D 重述的是他正在反驳的传统观点。
questions.passage: Historian Lucas Okonkwo's reassessment of the 1820s canal boom in the American interior challenges a familiar narrative. Conventional accounts credit canals with knitting isolated farm towns into a national market. Okonkwo does not dispute that canals lowered freight costs. But he marshals county shipping ledgers showing that many towns along the new waterways traded chiefly with one another rather than with distant cities, and that this local exchange, not long-distance commerce, accounted for most tonnage in the boom's first decade. The canals, he concludes, initially deepened regional bonds before they ever dissolved them.

choices.text: Canals failed to reduce the cost of shipping freight during the 1820s.
Canals at first intensified trade among nearby towns rather than immediately integrating them into a distant national market.
Canals had no measurable effect on the volume of goods shipped in their first decade.
Canals were valued by farm towns mainly because they connected those towns to large coastal cities.

questions.stem: Which choice most logically completes the text's implication about the coating?
questions.explanation:
The data isolate where the benefit lies: no faster warm-up, equally hot walls, but lower fuel use to 'hold a steady internal temperature,' with the benefit emerging only after hours of continuous running. The only consistent inference is that savings come from reduced heat loss during sustained operation (A). B contradicts 'just as hot to the touch.' C contradicts 'no faster.' D contradicts the benefit appearing only after several hours.

【中文】数据精确地定位了优势所在：升温不更快、外壁同样烫，但在“维持稳定内部温度”时耗油更少，且该优势仅在连续运行数小时后才显现。唯一一致的推断是节能来自持续运行期间减少的热量损失（A）。B 与“同样烫手”矛盾。C 与“升温不更快”矛盾。D 与优势仅在数小时后出现相矛盾。
questions.passage: Materials scientist Dario Belli tested a new ceramic coating intended to slow heat loss in industrial furnaces. In trials, furnaces lined with the coating reached operating temperature no faster than uncoated ones, and their walls grew just as hot to the touch. Yet over a full shift, the coated furnaces consumed measurably less fuel to hold a steady internal temperature. Belli noted that the coating's benefit appeared only once a furnace had been running continuously for several hours.

choices.text: The coating reduces fuel use chiefly by limiting heat loss during sustained operation rather than by speeding initial heating.
The coating prevents the outer walls of a furnace from becoming hot to the touch.
The coating allows furnaces to reach their operating temperature more quickly than uncoated furnaces.
The coating offers its greatest advantage during the first few minutes after a furnace is switched on.

questions.stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?
questions.explanation:
The study ties song complexity strongly to early-life food abundance and only weakly to present condition, and notes the most complex singers weren't necessarily healthiest at mating. The supported inference is that song reflects developmental history more than current health (A). B is unsupported—females still respond to song (males sing more elaborately mate earlier). C contradicts the opening correlation. D is an extreme absolute the data don't support.

【中文】研究将鸣声复杂度与幼年食物丰度强相关，与当前体况仅弱相关，并指出最复杂的鸣唱者在交配时不一定最健康。可支持的推断是：鸣声更多反映发育史而非当前健康（A）。B 缺乏支持——雌鸟仍对鸣声有反应（鸣声更复杂的雄鸟更早交配）。C 与开头的相关性矛盾。D 是数据不支持的极端绝对说法。
questions.passage: In many songbird species, males that sing more elaborate songs tend to secure mates earlier in the breeding season. Researchers long assumed elaborate song signaled a male's current vigor. But a recent long-term study found that song complexity in adult males correlated strongly with the abundance of food in the territory where each male had been raised as a nestling, and only weakly with the male's present body condition. The males with the most complex songs were not necessarily the healthiest at the time of mating.

choices.text: A male's song complexity may reflect the conditions of his early development more than his health at the moment a female evaluates him.
Female songbirds are unable to distinguish elaborate songs from simple ones.
Song complexity has no relationship to the timing of a male's mating success.
Males raised in food-poor territories never develop the ability to sing.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Reyes's skepticism about the claim that minimum-wage increases inevitably cut entry-level jobs?
questions.explanation:
Reyes's skepticism is about the word 'inevitably,' and her proposed test targets towns where a lone restaurant can pass costs to customers. A finding that, in exactly those towns, employment held steady because prices rose directly undercuts the universal claim—so A is the strongest support. B describes competitive markets, where the claim might still hold, so it doesn't challenge universality. C concerns profits, not the jobs claim. D is about customer preference, not actual employment outcomes.

【中文】Reyes 的怀疑针对的是“必然”一词，她提出的检验针对那些独家餐馆能将成本转嫁给顾客的城镇。如果恰恰在这些城镇，就业因涨价而保持稳定，就直接削弱了“普遍成立”的说法——因此 A 是最强的支持。B 描述的是竞争激烈的市场，那里该说法或许仍成立，故不挑战普遍性。C 关乎利润，而非就业说法。D 关乎顾客偏好，而非实际就业结果。
questions.passage: Some economists have argued that minimum-wage increases inevitably reduce the number of entry-level jobs offered by small restaurants. Economist Hana Reyes is skeptical of treating this outcome as universal. She points out that a restaurant's response depends heavily on whether it can pass higher labor costs on to customers—something easier in areas with little dining competition. To test whether the relationship truly holds everywhere, Reyes proposes examining how employment changed after wage increases specifically in towns where a single restaurant faces no nearby rivals.

choices.text: In towns where one restaurant had no nearby competitors, entry-level employment held steady after wage increases as the restaurants raised menu prices.
In cities with many competing restaurants, entry-level hiring fell sharply after each wage increase.
Restaurants in competitive areas reported the largest profit declines following wage increases.
Customers in towns with few restaurants said they would prefer lower menu prices.

questions.stem: Which quotation from Vohs's notebooks would best support the historian's interpretation?
questions.explanation:
The historian claims Vohs prevented flowering on purpose to preserve roots and leaves for study. Quotation A shows exactly that intent—she removes each bud so the plant directs strength to the roots she wants to examine—directly confirming deliberateness. B explains poor condition (failure narrative), not intent. C expresses a wish for flowers, contradicting the deliberate-prevention claim. D notes a soil difference, neutral about intent.

【中文】历史学家主张 Vohs 是故意阻止开花，以保留根和叶供研究。引文 A 正显示了这一意图——她摘除每一个花蕾，使植物将力量集中于她要研究的根——直接证实了其有意性。B 解释的是状况不佳（失败说），而非意图。C 表达了对花朵的渴望，与“故意阻止”相矛盾。D 指出土壤差异，对意图保持中立。
questions.passage: In her notebooks, the eighteenth-century botanist Margarethe Vohs described cultivating alpine gentians at low elevation. Modern readers have assumed she failed, since the plants she grew rarely flowered. A historian, however, contends that Vohs deliberately prevented flowering: she was studying the gentians' roots and leaves, and a flowering plant diverts resources away from those structures. The historian seeks textual evidence from Vohs's own notebooks to confirm that the absence of flowers was intentional rather than a sign of unsuccessful cultivation.

choices.text: "I pinch away each forming bud the moment it appears, that the plant may pour its whole strength into the roots I mean to examine."
"The gentians arrived from the mountains in poor condition, and many did not survive the journey to my garden."
"How I wish these stubborn plants would at last produce the brilliant blue blooms for which their kind is famed."
"The soil of the lowlands differs greatly from the thin alpine ground in which these plants naturally grow."

questions.stem: Which choice best uses data from the text to support the researchers' conclusion?
questions.explanation:
The conclusion is that endurance peaks at an intermediate color temperature and falls at the extremes. The data show 4000K highest (61) with both ends lower (2700K=52, 6500K=44), which is exactly the peak-in-the-middle pattern—so A supports the conclusion. B is false; values don't rise steadily and 6500K (44) is the lowest, not the maximum. C inverts the data. D is false: 52 ≠ 44.

【中文】结论是：耐力在中等色温处达到峰值，在两端下降。数据显示 4000K 最高（61），两端均更低（2700K=52，6500K=44），这正是“中间最高”的模式——故 A 支持结论。B 错误；数值并非稳步上升，且 6500K（44）是最低而非最高。C 颠倒了数据。D 错误：52 ≠ 44。
questions.passage: A team studied how reading light color affects how long readers could focus before reporting eye fatigue. Volunteers read under four light settings, and the team recorded the average minutes until first reported fatigue. Results: Warm (2700K), 52 minutes; Neutral (4000K), 61 minutes; Cool (5000K), 58 minutes; Very Cool (6500K), 44 minutes. The researchers concluded that focus endurance does not simply increase as light grows cooler; instead, it peaks at an intermediate color temperature and declines at the extremes.

choices.text: Endurance was highest at 4000K (61 minutes) and lower at both the warmest setting, 2700K (52 minutes), and the coolest, 6500K (44 minutes).
Endurance rose steadily from 2700K to 6500K, reaching its maximum at the coolest setting.
Endurance was lowest at 4000K and increased toward both the warmest and coolest settings.
Endurance was identical at 2700K and 6500K, the two most extreme settings.

questions.stem: Which choice best uses the data to identify the crop that best meets the lab's stated goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal balances substantial nitrogen against relatively low water use. Hairy vetch adds the most nitrogen (92) but at a steep water cost (420). Crimson clover adds the second-most nitrogen (78), beating field peas (64) and white lupin (71), while using far less water than vetch (310 vs 420)—the best trade-off, so A is correct. B ignores the water criterion. C optimizes only water and sacrifices nitrogen. D is factually false: white lupin (71) did not add more than every other crop.

【中文】目标是在“可观的氮”和“相对较低的耗水”之间取得平衡。毛苕子加氮最多（92）但耗水极高（420）。绛红三叶草加氮第二多（78），超过田豌豆（64）和白羽扇豆（71），且耗水远低于毛苕子（310 对 420）——这是最佳折中，故 A 正确。B 忽略了耗水标准。C 只优化耗水却牺牲了氮。D 事实错误：白羽扇豆（71）并非比其他所有作物都高。
questions.passage: An agricultural lab compared four cover crops for their ability to add nitrogen to depleted soil over one season. For each crop, researchers measured soil nitrogen added (in kilograms per hectare) and the crop's water use (in millimeters). Crimson clover added 78 kg/ha using 310 mm of water; field peas added 64 kg/ha using 290 mm; hairy vetch added 92 kg/ha using 420 mm; and white lupin added 71 kg/ha using 300 mm. The lab sought a crop that adds substantial nitrogen while keeping water use relatively low.

choices.text: Crimson clover, because it added 78 kg/ha—more nitrogen than field peas, white lupin, or any crop except hairy vetch—while using less water (310 mm) than hairy vetch (420 mm).
Hairy vetch, because it added the most nitrogen (92 kg/ha) regardless of its water use.
Field peas, because they used the least water (290 mm), even though they added the least nitrogen.
White lupin, because it added more nitrogen than any other crop while using only 300 mm of water.

questions.stem: As used in the text, what does the word "______" most nearly mean?
questions.explanation:
The text describes the staff shaping hundreds of loaves in hours, with hands moving in a "blur" so fast Maya "could barely follow" them. This presents the speed as striking and impressive, so "remarkable" fits best. "Gentle" and "ordinary" contradict the sense of astonishing speed, and nothing suggests the speed was "unwelcome."

【中文】文中描述员工在几小时内塑造数百个面包,双手快得像"模糊一片",玛雅"几乎跟不上"。这把这种速度描绘得惊人而令人印象深刻,因此"remarkable(非凡的)"最贴切。"gentle(温和的)"和"ordinary(普通的)"与惊人的速度相矛盾,文中也没有暗示这种速度"unwelcome(不受欢迎的)"。
questions.passage: Maya had always thought of bread-making as a slow, patient craft. But when she visited her grandmother's bakery during a holiday rush, she watched the staff shape hundreds of loaves in a matter of hours. The speed was ______; Maya could barely follow the blur of hands folding and rolling the dough.

choices.text: remarkable
gentle
unwelcome
ordinary

questions.stem: As used in the text, what does the word "______" most nearly mean?
questions.explanation:
The text says nearly every neighbor offered to help and that a waiting list filled within one afternoon, signaling strong, eager backing. "Enthusiastic" captures this eager support. "Reluctant" is the opposite, while "secret" and "temporary" do not match the openly offered, lasting commitment described.

【中文】文中说几乎每位邻居都主动帮忙,而且候补名单在一个下午就排满了,显示出强烈而热切的支持。"enthusiastic(热情的)"恰好体现这种热切支持。"reluctant(勉强的)"意思相反,而"secret(秘密的)"和"temporary(暂时的)"都与文中公开主动、持久的承诺不符。
questions.passage: When the city council proposed turning an empty parking lot into a community garden, residents responded with ______ support. Nearly every neighbor who attended the meeting offered to help plant, water, or donate seeds, and a waiting list for garden plots filled within a single afternoon.

choices.text: reluctant
enthusiastic
secret
temporary

questions.stem: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?
questions.explanation:
The text opens with a widely held assumption ("all bees live in large hives with a single queen"), then states that most bees are actually solitary and gives the mason bee as an illustrating example. This is a belief-then-counterexample structure, so A is correct. The text offers no solutions (B), does not follow one bee from birth (C), and mentions no competing scientists (D).

【中文】文章开头给出一个普遍假设("所有蜜蜂都生活在有一个蜂后的大蜂巢里"),接着指出大多数蜜蜂其实是独居的,并以泥瓦蜂为例加以说明。这是"普遍看法—反例"的结构,所以A正确。文中没有提供解决方案(B),没有从出生开始追踪一只蜜蜂(C),也没有提到相互竞争的科学家(D)。
questions.passage: Most people assume that all bees live in large hives with a single queen. In reality, the majority of bee species are solitary. A female mason bee, for example, builds her own small nest inside a hollow stem, lays her eggs, seals each chamber with mud, and then leaves. She never meets the offspring she has carefully provided for.

choices.text: It presents a common belief and then offers an example that complicates that belief.
It lists several solutions to a single environmental problem.
It traces the life cycle of one bee from birth to death.
It compares two scientists' competing theories about insects.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the text?
questions.explanation:
The text directly addresses new volunteers ("Before your first shift") and gives them practical steps to take so they can answer the public's questions confidently. Its purpose is to advise them on preparing for the role, so B is correct. It does not explain how art was made (A), argue for hiring (C), or recount museum history (D).

【中文】文章直接面向新志愿者("在你第一次值班之前"),并给出实用步骤,使他们能够自信地回答公众问题。其目的是指导他们为这份工作做准备,因此B正确。文中没有解释艺术品是如何创作的(A),没有主张增聘员工(C),也没有讲述博物馆历史(D)。
questions.passage:
The following text is from a guide written for new museum volunteers. 

Before your first shift, take time to walk slowly through every gallery. Read the labels, notice which artworks draw the most visitors, and ask the staff about pieces that puzzle you. The more familiar you are with the collection, the more confidently you will be able to answer questions from the public.

choices.text: To explain how a particular artwork was created
To advise new volunteers on how to prepare for their role
To argue that museums should hire more staff
To describe the history of a famous museum

questions.stem: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the claim about handwriting made in Text 1?
questions.explanation:
Text 1 treats the slowness of handwriting as a benefit because it forces summarizing. Text 2's author values typing precisely because it captures more of a lecture and notes that "handwriters scrambling to keep up often lose" key points. The Text 2 author would therefore counter that handwriting's slowness can cause students to miss important information, making B correct. A misstates Text 2's view, and C and D are far more extreme than anything Text 2 says.

【中文】文本1把手写的"慢"视为优点,因为它迫使学生概括。文本2的作者恰恰看重打字,因为它能记下更多讲课内容,并指出"手写者匆忙跟进时常常遗漏"要点。因此文本2的作者会反驳说:手写的慢会导致学生错过重要信息,所以B正确。A曲解了文本2的观点,而C和D都比文本2所说的极端得多。
questions.passage:
Text 1: Some education researchers argue that handwriting notes by hand helps students remember material better than typing. Because writing by hand is slower, students must summarize ideas in their own words rather than copying lectures word for word, and this active processing strengthens memory.

Text 2: For students who type quickly, laptops offer a clear advantage. They can capture more of a lecture, organize notes instantly, and search those notes later. In my experience teaching large classes, students who type rarely miss key points that handwriters scrambling to keep up often lose.

choices.text: By agreeing that slower note-taking always improves memory
By suggesting that the slowness of handwriting can cause students to miss important information
By recommending that students stop taking notes entirely
By claiming that no student should ever use a laptop in class

questions.stem: Which choice best describes a difference in how the two authors view the downtown bike lanes?
questions.explanation:
Text 1 calls the bike lanes "a success" and says traffic moves much as before, viewing them positively. Text 2 says they were "poorly planned" and caused longer backups and double-parking. So the key difference is positive view versus poorly-planned view, making A correct. B is wrong because Text 1 does not oppose cycling and Text 2 says "I support cycling." Neither focuses on cost (C), and neither concludes the lanes should be removed (D).

【中文】文本1称这些自行车道"是成功的",并说交通基本如旧,持正面看法。文本2则说它们"规划不当",造成更长的拥堵和违规停车。因此关键差异在于:正面评价对比"规划不当"的看法,所以A正确。B错,因为文本1并不反对骑车,而文本2明确说"我支持骑车"。两篇都没有聚焦成本(C),也都没有得出应当拆除车道的结论(D)。
questions.passage:
Text 1: The new bike lanes downtown have been a success. Since they opened, more residents are commuting by bicycle, and the streets feel calmer. Drivers have adjusted to the narrower lanes, and traffic moves much as it did before.

Text 2: While I support cycling, the downtown bike lanes were poorly planned. By removing a driving lane on Main Street, the city created longer backups during rush hour. Delivery trucks now double-park, blocking both cars and bicycles alike.

choices.text: Text 1 considers them an improvement, whereas Text 2 considers them poorly planned.
Text 1 opposes cycling, whereas Text 2 strongly favors it.
Text 1 focuses on cost, whereas Text 2 focuses on safety.
Both texts conclude that the lanes should be removed.

questions.stem: As used in the text, what does the word "varied" most nearly mean? (Select the choice that best fits the blank labeled "______.")
questions.explanation:
The blank describes a "buffet" that the following list explains: ornamental gardens, vacant lots, and planters that bloom in succession. The list emphasizes the wide range of nectar sources, so the missing word means having many different kinds. "Diverse" captures this sense of variety. "Unpredictable" and "shifting" stress change over time, but the point is the breadth of sources, not their instability; "decorative" wrongly narrows the meaning to appearance.

【中文】空格修饰后文解释的"buffet（自助餐式蜜源）"：观赏花园、空地和阳台花盆轮番开花。这一系列强调蜜源种类繁多，因此缺失的词意为"包含多种不同种类"。"diverse（多样的）"正合此意。"unpredictable（不可预测的）"和"shifting（不断变化的）"强调随时间变化，但重点是蜜源的广度而非不稳定；"decorative（装饰性的）"则错误地把含义局限于外观。
questions.passage: In her study of urban beekeeping, entomologist Priya Raman noted that rooftop hives in dense city centers often outperformed their rural counterparts. Far from being a hostile environment, the city offered bees a remarkably ______ buffet: ornamental gardens, weed-choked vacant lots, and balcony planters together bloomed in staggered succession across the seasons, so that nectar was rarely scarce.

choices.text: diverse
unpredictable
shifting
decorative

questions.stem: As used in the text, what does the word "trim" most nearly mean? (Select the choice that best fits the blank labeled "______.")
questions.explanation:
The next sentence confirms the meaning: the director "cut not a single scene," so the word he refused to do is reduce the length of his vision. "Shorten" matches this directly. "Decorate" and "polish" suggest improving or embellishing, not cutting; "scold" makes no sense applied to a "vision."

【中文】下一句证实了含义：导演"一个场景都没删"，所以他拒绝做的事是缩短自己的构想。"shorten（缩短）"与之直接对应。"decorate（装饰）"和"polish（润色）"意为改善或修饰，而非删减；"scold（责骂）"用于"vision（构想）"则讲不通。
questions.passage: When the small theater company announced it would stage a four-hour experimental drama with no intermission, critics predicted empty seats. The director, Marcus Oyelaran, refused to ______ his vision to accommodate restless audiences. He cut not a single scene, trusting that viewers willing to commit an entire evening would be rewarded rather than exhausted.

choices.text: decorate
scold
shorten
polish

questions.stem: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?
questions.explanation:
The text opens with a common belief (the press alone spread literacy), signaled by "It is tempting to credit" and "the story goes." The pivot "Yet" introduces evidence that schooling came first and mattered more, qualifying rather than rejecting the press's role. That is exactly choice A. B misreads the text as a neutral history; C invents a second technology; D wrongly frames it as a definition with examples.

【中文】文本以一种常见看法（仅靠印刷机就普及了识字）开头，由"It is tempting to credit"和"the story goes"标示。转折词"Yet"引出证据：学校教育更早出现且作用更大，从而是"修正"而非完全否定印刷机的作用。这正是选项A。B把文本误读为中立的历史叙述；C凭空捏造第二种技术；D错误地将其框定为下定义加举例。
questions.passage: It is tempting to credit the printing press alone with the spread of literacy in early modern Europe. Cheap books, the story goes, simply created their own readers. Yet literacy rates climbed fastest in regions where local councils had already funded parish schools decades before any press arrived. The machine mattered, but it accelerated a process that schooling, not technology, had set in motion.

choices.text: It presents a widely held explanation and then qualifies it by identifying a prior, more decisive cause.
It traces the invention of a technology and lists the regions where it was first adopted.
It compares two competing technologies and argues that both were equally important.
It defines a historical term and then provides several examples of its use.

questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the last sentence of the text?
questions.explanation:
The final sentence ("less a memorial than a map") restates Forsberg's point that the crack revealed the alloy's weakness and redirected her research. It reframes the tile from a humbling reminder of failure into something useful that guided her work, which is choice B. A contradicts her positive framing; C contradicts her statement that the fracture "redirected three years"; D introduces chemistry the text never discusses.

【中文】最后一句（"与其说是纪念物，不如说是一张地图"）重申了Forsberg的观点：裂缝揭示了合金最薄弱之处并改变了她的研究方向。它把瓷砖从一个令人谦卑的失败提醒，重新定义为指引其工作的有用之物，即选项B。A与她的正面解读相矛盾；C与她"改变了三年研究方向"的说法相矛盾；D引入了文中从未涉及的化学过程。
questions.passage:
The following text is from a profile of materials scientist Dr. Lena Forsberg.

Forsberg keeps a cracked ceramic tile on her desk, a souvenir from an experiment that failed spectacularly. "People assume I display it to stay humble," she says. "Actually, I keep it because the crack told me exactly where the alloy was weakest. That fracture redirected three years of my research." The tile, in other words, is less a memorial than a map.

choices.text: To suggest that Forsberg regrets the failed experiment that produced the tile
To reframe the tile's significance, recasting an apparent symbol of failure as a source of guidance
To question whether the tile actually influenced Forsberg's later research
To explain the chemical process that caused the ceramic tile to crack

questions.stem: Based on the texts, how would Achebe (Text 2) most likely respond to the argument made by the "proponents" in Text 1?
questions.explanation:
Achebe explicitly "acknowledges that remote workers report being happier," so he concedes the satisfaction point the proponents emphasize. But he warns that "happiness is not the same as effectiveness" and cites slower onboarding and fewer shared ideas, costs surveys miss. That is the concede-then-qualify response in choice A. B contradicts his acknowledgment; C and D adopt positions the proponents hold, not Achebe's critique.

【中文】Achebe明确"承认远程员工报告更幸福"，因此他认同支持者强调的满意度这一点。但他警告"幸福不等于效能"，并列举上手培训更慢、自发想法更少等被满意度调查忽略的代价。这正是选项A中"先承认再修正"的回应方式。B与他的承认相矛盾；C和D采取的是支持者的立场，而非Achebe的批评。
questions.passage:
Text 1: Proponents of remote work point to commuting time saved and to surveys in which employees report higher satisfaction. When workers control their own schedules, supporters argue, they protect their focus from the constant interruptions of an open office, and productivity rises as a result.

Text 2: Researcher Daniel Achebe acknowledges that remote workers report being happier. But happiness, he cautions, is not the same as effectiveness. His team found that fully remote employees, though content, took significantly longer to onboard new colleagues and shared fewer spontaneous ideas, costs that satisfaction surveys simply fail to capture.

choices.text: By agreeing that remote workers are more satisfied but arguing that satisfaction overlooks hidden costs to collaboration
By denying that remote workers report any increase in satisfaction
By insisting that open offices interrupt focus even more than the proponents claim
By concluding that saved commuting time is the most important benefit of remote work

questions.stem: Which choice best describes the relationship between the two texts?
questions.explanation:
Text 1 issues a flat warning that ivy damages walls. Text 2 offers a "more measured verdict": on sound walls ivy is protective, and harm appears only where the wall was already failing. This does not fully confirm (B) or entirely reject (C) Text 1; rather, it qualifies the warning by showing it holds only in some cases. That nuance-adding relationship is choice A. D is wrong because Text 2 changes the position, not just the vocabulary.

【中文】文本1发出绝对警告：常春藤会损坏墙体。文本2给出"更为审慎的结论"：在完好的墙上常春藤反而起保护作用，损害只出现在本已破损的墙上。这既不是完全证实（B），也不是彻底否定（C）文本1，而是通过表明该警告仅在部分情形下成立来加以限定。这种"增加细微差别"的关系即选项A。D错误，因为文本2改变的是立场，而不仅仅是用词。
questions.passage:
Text 1: Many garden writers warn against planting ivy on a house wall. Its clinging roots, they insist, pry into mortar joints and crumbling brick, accelerating decay and trapping the very moisture that masonry needs to release.

Text 2: A recent survey of historic buildings by architect Sofia Renaldi reached a more measured verdict. On sound, well-pointed walls, she reports, an ivy cover actually buffered the masonry against rain and temperature swings. Damage appeared only where the wall was already failing; ivy did not create the weakness so much as it exploited weakness that was already there.

choices.text: Text 2 presents evidence that complicates the blanket warning offered in Text 1.
Text 2 provides additional examples that fully confirm the claim in Text 1.
Text 2 rejects the central concern of Text 1 as entirely baseless.
Text 2 restates the position of Text 1 using more technical vocabulary.

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?
questions.explanation:
The passage frames the flourishes as 'ornamental conventions inherited from earlier engravers and reproduced almost without thought,' and the final sentence contrasts 'the habits of a craft' with 'the convictions of a science.' The missing word must mean automatic or done from habit without deliberate intent. 'Rote' (mechanical, habitual, performed without thought) fits exactly. 'Audacious' (bold) and 'meticulous' (carefully precise) both imply deliberate intent, the opposite of 'without thought.' 'Contested' (disputed) describes the naturalists' reaction, not the nature of the gestures themselves.

【中文】文段把这些花饰描述为'从早期雕版师那里继承下来、几乎不假思索地重复'的'装饰性惯例'，末句又把'技艺的习惯'与'科学的信念'相对比。所填词须表示'机械的、出于习惯而非有意为之'。'rote'（机械重复的、出于习惯而不经思考的）恰好符合。'audacious'（大胆的）和'meticulous'（一丝不苟的）都暗示刻意为之，与'不假思索'相反。'contested'（有争议的）描述的是博物学家的反应，而非这些花饰本身的性质。
questions.passage: In her study of eighteenth-century botanical illustrators, art historian Priya Nandakumar resists the temptation to read every stylistic flourish as a deliberate scientific claim. Many of the curling tendrils and exaggerated blooms that later naturalists derided as inaccuracies, she argues, were not errors of observation but ______ gestures—ornamental conventions inherited from earlier engravers and reproduced almost without thought. To indict the illustrators for imprecision, on this view, is to mistake the habits of a craft for the convictions of a science.

choices.text: rote
audacious
contested
meticulous

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?
questions.explanation:
The clause that follows defines the missing word: 'a draft that the act of publication merely paused rather than concluded,' and the last sentence states 'print conferred no finality.' The word must mean temporary or not final. 'Provisional' (temporary; subject to later change) is precise. 'Definitive' means final, the exact opposite of the context. 'Derivative' (imitative) and 'scandalous' (shocking) are unrelated to the idea of incompleteness being described.

【中文】随后的从句对所填词作了定义：'一份草稿，出版只是让它暂停而非结束'，末句也说'印刷并未赋予其终局性'。该词须表示'临时的、非最终的'。'provisional'（临时的、可后续更改的）最精确。'definitive'意为'最终的'，与语境正相反。'derivative'（模仿的）和'scandalous'（骇人的）都与此处所描述的'未完成'之意无关。
questions.passage: The novelist Teodora Vasilescu was notorious for revising her published work, but her motives have been misread. Critics long assumed she rewrote out of dissatisfaction, treating each new edition as a confession that the previous one had failed. Yet her letters suggest something stranger: she regarded a finished book not as a verdict but as a ______ proposition, a draft that the act of publication merely paused rather than concluded. For Vasilescu, print conferred no finality.

choices.text: definitive
provisional
derivative
scandalous

questions.stem: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?
questions.explanation:
The text opens with a common claim ('the printing press democratized knowledge by making books cheap'), grants it is 'partly true,' then pivots with 'But' to argue the real significance was standardization rather than access. That is exactly the structure in A: common claim, partial concession, redirection to an overlooked factor. B is wrong because no archival evidence is introduced. C is wrong because the author endorses one factor (standardization), not rejects both. D is wrong because no multi-century evolution of opinion is traced, and the author does not endorse the original 'access' view.

【中文】文段先提出一个常见说法（'印刷术通过让书变便宜而使知识民主化'），承认它'部分正确'，再用'But'转折，论证真正的意义在于标准化而非获取渠道。这正是选项A的结构：常见说法—部分让步—转向被忽视的因素。B错，因为文中并未引入档案证据。C错，因为作者认可了其中一个因素（标准化），并非否定两者。D错，因为文中没有追溯跨数百年的观点演变，作者也并未认可最初的'获取渠道'说法。
questions.passage: It is often said that the printing press democratized knowledge by making books cheap. The claim is appealing, and partly true. But in the first century after Gutenberg, printed books remained expensive, and literacy rates barely moved. What the press changed first was not who could read but what readers could compare: for the first time, two distant scholars could be confident they were examining identical texts, word for word. The revolution, in other words, lay less in access than in standardization—a precondition the access narrative tends to overlook.

choices.text: It presents a widely held claim, partially concedes its merit, and then redirects attention to a different and overlooked factor.
It introduces a historical puzzle and resolves it by appealing to newly discovered archival evidence.
It compares two competing theories and demonstrates that both are fundamentally mistaken.
It traces a gradual change in scholarly opinion over several centuries before endorsing the earliest view.

questions.stem: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?
questions.explanation:
The text invites a reader to see the pupfish as 'supremely adapted, a triumph of natural selection,' then reframes that interpretation: its tolerance is 'a record of confinement than of conquest,' and its resilience is 'the resilience of the trapped.' The purpose is to reinterpret an apparent success as a result of being stranded—exactly B. A states the very view the text overturns. C introduces a threat-and-warning purpose the passage never raises. D names a detail (the lakes) but mistakes a supporting fact for the overall purpose.

【中文】文段先引导读者把沙漠鳉鱼看作'极度适应、自然选择的胜利'，再重新诠释：它的耐受性是'被困的记录而非征服的记录'，其韧性是'被困者的韧性'。其目的是把表面的成功重新解读为被困的结果，正是选项B。A正是文段所推翻的看法。C引入了一个文中从未提出的'威胁与警示'目的。D提到一个细节（湖泊），却把支撑性事实误当作整体目的。
questions.passage: Consider the desert pupfish, which survives in pools so saline and warm that almost no other vertebrate can endure them. One might assume such a creature is supremely adapted, a triumph of natural selection. But its tolerance is better understood as a record of confinement than of conquest: the pupfish thrives in lethal water only because it was stranded there as larger lakes evaporated, with nowhere milder to go. Its resilience is real, yet it is the resilience of the trapped.

choices.text: To argue that the desert pupfish is more impressively adapted than other vertebrates are
To recast an apparent sign of evolutionary success as the consequence of being trapped
To warn that rising salinity threatens the survival of the desert pupfish
To compare the pupfish's habitat with the larger lakes from which it descends

questions.stem: Based on the texts, how would Delgado (Text 2) most likely respond to the use of satisfaction surveys described in Text 1?
questions.explanation:
Text 1 treats high-satisfaction surveys as confirming that gig workers genuinely enjoy autonomy. Delgado in Text 2 says workers 'report contentment, but their behavior tells another story'—they accept almost every assignment because earnings are too unpredictable to refuse, so 'the freedom to decline exists on paper while economic necessity quietly dictates the schedule.' She would therefore argue the surveys hide the gap between professed freedom and actual constrained behavior—choice B. A contradicts her skepticism. D misstates her view: she does not dispute that workers report contentment, only what that contentment proves. C introduces an expansion she never suggests.

【中文】文一把高满意度调查视为'印证'零工真正享有自主权。文二中的Delgado指出工人'报告满意，但他们的行为却讲述了另一个故事'——他们几乎接受每一份工作，因为收入太不稳定而不敢拒绝，所以'拒绝的自由停留在纸面上，而经济需要悄悄地支配着工作时间'。因此她会主张这些调查掩盖了'声称的自由'与'实际受限行为'之间的落差，即选项B。A与她的怀疑态度相矛盾。D错误地表述了她的观点：她并不否认工人报告满意，而只是质疑这种满意能证明什么。C引入了她从未提出的'扩大调查'之说。
questions.passage:
Text 1: Economists studying the gig economy frequently celebrate its flexibility. Workers, they note, can choose their own hours and decline assignments at will. This autonomy, the argument goes, represents a genuine gain over the rigid schedules of traditional employment, and surveys showing high worker satisfaction are taken as confirmation.

Text 2: Sociologist Mara Delgado is unconvinced by such satisfaction surveys. Workers report contentment, she observes, but their behavior tells another story: most accept nearly every assignment offered, regardless of timing, because earnings are too unpredictable to refuse work. The freedom to decline, in her account, exists on paper while economic necessity quietly dictates the schedule.

choices.text: By agreeing that the surveys accurately capture the autonomy gig workers enjoy
By contending that the surveys obscure a gap between workers' stated freedom and their constrained behavior
By proposing that the surveys be expanded to include workers in traditional employment
By insisting that gig workers are, on the whole, less satisfied than the surveys report

questions.stem: Based on the texts, Adeyemi (Text 2) would most likely characterize the success criterion described in Text 1 as
questions.explanation:
Text 1's criterion judges restoration by resemblance to a documented historical baseline. Adeyemi 'questions the assumption that the past should govern,' notes that shifted climate and soils may make a once-flourishing community 'untenable,' and argues the real test is self-sustainability 'under present and future conditions,' not a match to old records. She would thus see the Text 1 criterion as misguided for anchoring success to a past state current conditions may not support—choice B. A endorses the historical standard she rejects. C faults the criterion for vagueness about species lists, but her objection is to the use of the past at all, not to missing details. D concedes the criterion is sound and only objects on cost, which misrepresents her substantive disagreement.

【中文】文一的标准以'是否与有记录的历史基线相似'来评判修复是否成功。Adeyemi'质疑应以过去为准的假设'，指出气候和土壤已经改变，曾经繁盛的群落如今可能'无法维持'，并主张真正的检验是能否在'当前及未来条件下'自我维持，而非与旧记录相符。因此她会认为文一的标准是错误的，因为它把成功锚定在当前条件可能不再支持的过去状态上，即选项B。A认可了她所否定的历史标准。C指责该标准没有明确物种清单，但她反对的是'以过去为准'本身，而非细节缺失。D承认该标准本身合理、只在成本上反对，这歪曲了她实质性的分歧。
questions.passage:
Text 1: In debates over restoring degraded grasslands, many ecologists advocate setting a historical baseline—the species composition recorded before heavy human disturbance—and managing the land to return it to that state. A restoration is judged successful, on this approach, to the degree that the recovered community resembles the documented past.

Text 2: Ecologist Onyeka Adeyemi questions the assumption that the past should govern. Climate and soils have shifted, she notes, and a community that once flourished may now be untenable. The pertinent question, she argues, is not whether a restored grassland matches old records but whether it can sustain itself and its functions under present and future conditions.

choices.text: useful precisely because historical records provide the only objective standard available to restoration ecologists
misguided in tying success to a past state that current conditions may no longer support
incomplete because it fails to specify which species were present before human disturbance
sound in principle but too costly to implement on degraded grasslands

questions.stem: The student wants to emphasize an unusual nesting habit of the Carolina wren. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal is to emphasize an unusual nesting habit. Choice B directly describes how the wren builds nests in flowerpots and mailboxes and signals that this is uncommon with 'Unlike many other songbirds.' Choices A, C, and D describe the bird's weight, appearance, and range, not its nesting habit.

【中文】题目要求强调卡罗莱纳鹪鹩不寻常的筑巢习性。选项B直接描述它在花盆和邮箱里筑巢，并用'与许多其他鸣禽不同'点明这一行为不寻常。选项A、C、D分别讲体重、外观和分布范围，与筑巢习性无关。
questions.passage:
While researching local birds, a student took the following notes:

- The Carolina wren is a small songbird native to the eastern United States.
- It has reddish-brown feathers and a white stripe above each eye.
- Unlike many songbirds, both the male and female sing.
- The bird weighs about 20 grams.
- It often builds its nest inside flowerpots, mailboxes, and other human-made objects.

choices.text: The Carolina wren, a small songbird, weighs only about 20 grams.
Unlike many other songbirds, the Carolina wren often builds its nest inside human-made objects such as flowerpots and mailboxes.
The Carolina wren has reddish-brown feathers and a white stripe above each eye.
The Carolina wren is a small songbird native to the eastern United States.

questions.stem: The student wants to highlight the garden's contribution to feeding people in need. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal is to highlight the garden's role in feeding people in need. Choice C states that volunteers donated more than 1,200 pounds of vegetables to a local food bank, directly addressing that goal. The other choices focus on the opening date, the lot's location, or neighborhood friendliness.

【中文】题目要求突出花园在帮助有需要的人获得食物方面的贡献。选项C说明志愿者向当地食物银行捐赠了超过1200磅蔬菜，正好契合该目标。其他选项分别关注开放时间、地块位置或邻里氛围，均不相关。
questions.passage:
A student is writing about a community garden and has compiled these notes:

- The Maple Street Community Garden opened in 2019.
- It is located on an empty lot that was once filled with trash.
- Volunteers grow vegetables and donate them to a local food bank.
- In 2023, the garden donated more than 1,200 pounds of food.
- Neighbors say the garden has made the street feel friendlier.

choices.text: The Maple Street Community Garden opened in 2019 on a once-trash-filled lot.
Neighbors say the Maple Street Community Garden has made their street feel friendlier.
In 2023, volunteers at the garden donated more than 1,200 pounds of vegetables to a local food bank.
The garden is located on an empty lot on Maple Street.

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
The first sentence states a common belief (bats are blind); the second sentence contradicts it (bats can see well). A contrast transition is needed, so 'However' is correct. 'Therefore' shows a result, 'For example' introduces an illustration, and 'Similarly' shows likeness—none fit the contrast.

【中文】第一句陈述一种普遍看法（蝙蝠是瞎的）；第二句与之相反（蝙蝠视力其实很好）。这里需要表示转折的连接词，因此'However'（然而）正确。'Therefore'表结果，'For example'引出例子，'Similarly'表相似，都不符合转折关系。
questions.passage: Many people think that all bats are blind. ______ bats can actually see quite well, and some species use their eyes more than they use sound to find their way at night.

choices.text: However,
Therefore,
For example,
Similarly,

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
The first sentence gives a goal (saving money for a bicycle), and the second gives the action Elena took to reach it (packing her lunch). This is a cause-and-effect relationship, so 'So' is correct. 'Nevertheless' and 'In contrast' signal opposition, and 'Meanwhile' signals simultaneous time, none of which fit.

【中文】第一句给出目标（攒钱买自行车），第二句给出她为实现目标所采取的行动（自带午餐）。这是因果关系，因此'So'（所以）正确。'Nevertheless'和'In contrast'表对比，'Meanwhile'表同时发生，都不符合。
questions.passage: Elena wanted to save money for a new bicycle. ______ she began packing her lunch instead of buying it at school every day. Within a few months, she had saved enough to buy the bike she wanted.

choices.text: Nevertheless,
So,
In contrast,
Meanwhile,

questions.stem: The student wants to emphasize a drawback of urban beekeeping. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal is to emphasize a drawback. Choice B presents the critics' concern, that crowding too many hives into one area starves wild pollinators, as the main point, which is a clear drawback. Choices A, C, and D all describe benefits of urban beekeeping, not drawbacks.

【中文】目标是强调一个缺点。选项B把批评者的担忧——在一个区域内放置过多蜂箱会使野生传粉者得不到花蜜——作为重点呈现,这是明确的缺点。选项A、C、D描述的都是城市养蜂的好处,而不是缺点。
questions.passage:
While researching urban beekeeping, a student took the following notes:

- Rooftop hives have become common in several large cities.
- Bees from these hives pollinate plants in nearby parks and community gardens.
- One survey found that neighborhoods with rooftop hives had 18 percent more flowering plants than nearby neighborhoods without hives.
- Critics note that too many hives in one area can leave little nectar for wild pollinators.

choices.text: Rooftop hives have become common in several large cities, where bees pollinate nearby parks and gardens.
Although rooftop hives can increase the number of flowering plants in a neighborhood, packing too many hives into one area can leave little nectar for wild pollinators.
Neighborhoods with rooftop hives had 18 percent more flowering plants than nearby neighborhoods without hives.
Bees from rooftop hives pollinate plants in nearby parks and community gardens, helping those spaces flourish.

questions.stem: The student wants to present Dr. Okafor's findings to an audience already familiar with how salt marshes work. Which choice most effectively uses the relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The audience already knows how salt marshes work, so background facts (A and B) are unnecessary. The goal is to present the findings. Choice C reports the actual results, the planted area gained 4 cm while the control lost 2 cm, which is the finding. Choice D mentions the study but omits any results.

【中文】受众已经了解盐沼的运作方式,因此背景知识(A和B)没有必要。目标是呈现研究结果。选项C报告了真实结果:种植区升高了4厘米,而对照区下降了2厘米,这正是研究发现。选项D提到了研究但没有给出任何结果。
questions.passage:
While studying a coastal restoration project, a student took the following notes:

- Salt marshes are wetlands that flood with seawater at high tide.
- Marsh grasses trap sediment, which slowly raises the height of the marsh.
- In 2019, ecologist Dr. Lena Okafor planted marsh grasses along an eroding shoreline in a study area.
- By 2023, the study area had gained an average of 4 centimeters in elevation, while an unplanted control area lost 2 centimeters.

choices.text: Salt marshes are wetlands that flood with seawater at high tide, and their grasses trap sediment over time.
Marsh grasses trap sediment, which slowly raises the height of the marsh in which they grow.
After Dr. Okafor planted marsh grasses along an eroding shoreline in 2019, the study area gained an average of 4 centimeters in elevation by 2023, while an unplanted control area lost 2 centimeters.
Dr. Okafor, an ecologist, studied an eroding shoreline beginning in 2019.

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
The first sentence describes a negative situation: rejection and lost manuscripts. The second sentence describes a reversal: the scores were found and interest revived. This contrast calls for 'However.' 'Therefore' signals a result, 'Likewise' signals similarity, and 'For instance' signals an example, none of which fit the contrast.

【中文】第一句描述的是负面情况:遭拒绝、手稿遗失。第二句描述的是转折:乐谱被发现,人们重新产生兴趣。这种对比需要用"However(然而)"。"Therefore"表示结果,"Likewise"表示相似,"For instance"表示举例,都不符合这种对比关系。
questions.passage: The composer Florence Price faced repeated rejection from orchestras during her lifetime, and many of her manuscripts were thought to be lost. ______ in 2009, renovators of an abandoned house she once owned discovered boxes of her unpublished scores, prompting a major revival of interest in her music.

choices.text: Therefore,
However,
Likewise,
For instance,

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
Both sentences describe measures the engineers took to reduce sway: first the tuned mass dampers, then the shaped deck. The second measure is added to the first, so 'In addition' fits. 'Nevertheless' and 'By contrast' signal opposition, and 'As a result' signals an effect, but the deck shaping is a second solution, not a consequence of the dampers.

【中文】两句话都描述工程师为减少摇晃所采取的措施:先是调谐质量阻尼器,然后是经过造型的桥面。第二项措施是在第一项基础上的补充,因此用"In addition(此外)"最合适。"Nevertheless"和"By contrast"表示对立,"As a result"表示结果,但桥面造型是第二个解决方案,而不是阻尼器带来的结果。
questions.passage: Engineers designing the new pedestrian bridge needed it to withstand strong winds without swaying uncomfortably. They added a set of heavy weights, called tuned mass dampers, that move opposite to the bridge's motion and cancel out the sway. ______ they shaped the underside of the deck to let wind pass smoothly rather than catch against it.

choices.text: Nevertheless,
In addition,
By contrast,
As a result,

questions.stem: The student wants to emphasize a difference between the two dinosaurs' likely hunting adaptations. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal is to emphasize a DIFFERENCE in likely HUNTING adaptations. Choice B contrasts T. rex's binocular vision (good for judging distance to prey) with Tarbosaurus's shallow sockets and inferred reliance on smell—directly a hunting-adaptation contrast. A states a similarity, not a difference. C contrasts geography, not hunting. D describes only Tarbosaurus and mentions no hunting adaptation or contrast.

【中文】目标是强调两者在捕猎适应上的差异。选项B将霸王龙的双眼视觉（有助于判断与猎物的距离）与特暴龙浅眼窝及推断其更依赖嗅觉进行对比，正是捕猎适应方面的差异。A陈述的是相似点而非差异。C对比的是地理分布，与捕猎无关。D只描述特暴龙，没有提到捕猎适应，也没有对比。
questions.passage:
While researching the Tarbosaurus, a paleontology student took the following notes:

- Tarbosaurus bataar was a large carnivorous dinosaur that lived in what is now Mongolia.
- It is closely related to the North American Tyrannosaurus rex.
- Many fossil skulls of Tarbosaurus show a narrow snout and shallow eye sockets.
- By contrast, T. rex skulls have broad snouts and forward-facing eyes that allowed binocular vision.
- Binocular vision improves depth perception, useful for judging distance to prey.
- Some researchers infer that Tarbosaurus relied more on its sense of smell than on stereoscopic sight when hunting.

choices.text: Both Tarbosaurus bataar and Tyrannosaurus rex were large carnivores, and the two species were closely related.
Unlike T. rex, whose forward-facing eyes provided depth-enhancing binocular vision, Tarbosaurus had shallow eye sockets, leading some researchers to infer it relied more on smell when hunting.
Tarbosaurus bataar lived in what is now Mongolia, whereas its relative T. rex lived in North America.
Many fossil skulls of Tarbosaurus, a relative of T. rex, have been found and show a narrow snout.

questions.stem: The student wants to present the trial's findings and explain why the untreated buildings were included. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
questions.explanation:
The goal has two parts: present the findings AND explain why untreated buildings were included. Choice B reports both findings (3.2°C cooler, 18% less energy) and explains the untreated buildings' role as a comparison group, noting they were similar—the basis for valid comparison. A defines cool roofs but gives no findings. C mentions both building groups but states no findings and no rationale. D gives findings but overgeneralizes and never explains the untreated group.

【中文】目标有两部分：呈现结果，并解释为何纳入未处理的建筑。选项B既报告了两项结果（室内低3.2°C、能耗减少18%），又说明未处理建筑作为对照组的作用，并指出其条件相似，是有效对比的基础。A定义了凉爽屋顶但没有结果。C提到两组建筑但既无结果也无理由。D给出结果却过度概括，且从未解释对照组。
questions.passage:
While studying urban heat, a student took the following notes:

- A "cool roof" is coated with reflective material to bounce sunlight away.
- In a 2021 trial, a city retrofitted 40 buildings with cool roofs.
- Indoor temperatures in retrofitted buildings averaged 3.2°C lower on hot days.
- Energy used for air conditioning in those buildings dropped 18 percent.
- A separate group of 40 untreated buildings showed no change in either measure.
- The retrofitted and untreated buildings were of similar size and age.

choices.text: Cool roofs, which are coated with reflective material, bounce sunlight away and can be installed on buildings of any size or age.

In the 2021 trial, retrofitted buildings averaged 3.2°C cooler indoors and used 18 percent less air-conditioning energy; the 40 similar untreated buildings, which showed no change, served as a comparison group.
The city retrofitted 40 buildings with cool roofs in 2021 and kept another 40 buildings untreated.
Because indoor temperatures fell 3.2°C and energy use dropped 18 percent, cool roofs are clearly worth installing in every city.

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
Velasco ASSUMED her work would merely preserve a dying language; what she actually found—teenagers reviving it in song—contradicts that expectation. "Instead" signals that the reality replaced the anticipated outcome. "Therefore" marks a result, not a reversal. "Likewise" signals similarity, but the two ideas conflict. "In addition" merely adds, missing the contrast between expectation and discovery.

【中文】贝拉斯科原以为她的工作只是保存一门濒死的语言；而她实际发现的——青少年用歌曲使其复活——与该预期相矛盾。"Instead"（相反）表示现实取代了预期结果。"Therefore"表结果，而非反转。"Likewise"表相似，但两个观点是冲突的。"In addition"只是补充，未能体现预期与发现之间的对比。
questions.passage: Linguist Mara Velasco set out to document Tz'ut, a language spoken by fewer than two hundred people in a remote valley. Most of those speakers were over sixty, and almost no children were learning the language at home. Velasco assumed her recordings would simply preserve a vanishing tongue. ______ during her fieldwork she discovered a small group of teenagers using Tz'ut in newly invented song lyrics, suggesting the language might be acquiring an unexpected second life.

choices.text: Therefore,
Instead,
Likewise,
In addition,

questions.stem: Which choice completes the text with the most logical transition?
questions.explanation:
Harnett's employment stayed FLAT (a positive result for the policy); Calder's employment FELL SHARPLY—an opposite outcome. "By contrast" signals this opposition between the two towns. "For instance" would make Calder an example of the same pattern, but it is the opposite. "As a result" implies Calder's drop was caused by Harnett's result, which is illogical. "In conclusion" signals a summary, but new contrasting data is being introduced.

【中文】哈尼特的就业保持平稳（对政策而言是正面结果）；而卡尔德的就业大幅下降——是相反的结果。"By contrast"（相比之下）表明两镇之间的这种对立。"For instance"会让卡尔德成为同一模式的例子，但它恰恰相反。"As a result"暗示卡尔德的下降由哈尼特的结果导致，不合逻辑。"In conclusion"表总结，但此处引入的是新的对比数据。
questions.passage: Economist Priya Nadeem analyzed wages in two neighboring towns after each raised its minimum wage. In Harnett, employment in restaurants stayed flat for a year, which critics had predicted would not happen. The result seemed to vindicate the policy.
